title: Context Limits of Few-Shot
concept: In-context learning is fast but limited by the model's context window; long prompt examples consume token budgets, prompting fine-tuning for high-volume tasks. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning.'

exercises.prompt: What is the primary way we show examples in 'few-shot' learning?

options.text: Providing examples directly inside the chat prompt
Retraining the entire AI model from scratch on supercomputers
Connecting the AI to a robot body

exercises.explain: Few-shot learning lets you teach the AI on the fly by providing examples directly inside your input prompt.

cards.title: Hitting the Wall
cards.body: Few-shot learning lets us teach an AI new tricks in seconds just by giving it a few examples. But what happens when we have hundreds of examples to show it? We run straight into the brick wall of 'context limits'! Let's explore why prompts can get too crowded.

cards.title: What is the Context Limit?
cards.body: Few-shot learning relies on 'in-context learning'. This means the AI learns temporarily from examples you put directly inside your chat prompt. However, because LLMs have a strict limit on how much text they can read at once (the context window), cramming too many long examples into a prompt will quickly run out of space.

cards.title: The Small Whiteboard
cards.body: Imagine a student taking an open-book exam. They can quickly look at a few solved examples written on a small whiteboard next to them to solve new problems. This works great! But if they need fifty examples, the whiteboard runs out of space. To handle that many, the student needs to actually study and memorize the patterns beforehand—which is like fine-tuning.

cards.title: A Tight Fit
cards.body: If you want an AI to translate slang, you might give it three examples in your prompt: 'No cap -> No lie', 'Bet -> Okay', and 'Slay -> Do great'. This fits easily. But if you try to paste a 500-page slang dictionary into the prompt, the AI will crash or ignore the text because you exceeded its context limit. For that, you would want to fine-tune the model.

cards.title: The Unlimited Prompt Myth
cards.body: Some believe that if an AI has a huge context window, we should always use few-shot learning instead of fine-tuning. In reality, processing massive prompts with dozens of examples makes every single question slower and much more expensive to run. Fine-tuning builds the knowledge directly into the AI's brain!

cards.title: Two Ways to Learn
cards.body: See how few-shot learning stores temporary examples in the prompt, while fine-tuning bakes them directly into the AI's brain.

left.text: Examples are pasted into the prompt. Fast to set up, but limited by how much text can fit.

right.text: Examples are trained directly into the model's weights. Unlimited scale, but takes time to train.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you put too many long examples in a few-shot prompt?

options.text: It makes the AI completely lose its ability to speak English.
It consumes the token budget, leaving less room for your actual question.
It forces the AI to permanently delete its core training data.
It instantly turns the AI into a human developer.

exercises.explain: The 'context window' acts as the memory limit for a single prompt. If you put too many examples in, you run out of space for new questions and answers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Few-shot learning is limited by the maximum amount of text (context window) the AI can read at one time.
exercises.explain: Few-shot learning is fast because it happens in the prompt without modifying the AI's core weights, but it is strictly limited by the context window.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: If your examples exceed the prompt limit, you should switch to...
exercises.sentence: To teach an AI using thousands of examples without overloading the context window, you should use fine-tuning.
exercises.explain: When your examples are too long or numerous for the context window, you should use fine-tuning to train the AI's core weights instead of using few-shot prompts.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs shows a failure caused by exceeding the context limit?

options.text: Input: 'Translate: Hello -> Bonjour. Translate: Cat -> Chat.' Output: 'Chat'
Input: '[100 pages of examples] Translate: Dog ->' Output: '[Error: Context window exceeded]'

exercises.explain: The third option shows the AI hallucinating or outputting an error because the context limit was cut off, which is a classic symptom of exceeding the token limit with too many examples.

exercises.prompt: Why is fine-tuning often preferred over few-shot learning when you need to teach an AI a massive amount of examples?

options.text: Because few-shot learning permanently updates the AI's internal weights.
Because long examples consume the context window, leaving less room for the actual prompt and response.
Because few-shot learning is too slow to process even a single example.
Because the AI forgets its original training when you show it examples.

exercises.explain: Fantastic job! You've mastered how context limits shape our choice between few-shot learning and fine-tuning. Next up: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning.
